topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the line of the circuit board with a fingertip, a familiar ache blooming in her chest. She leaned back, stretching her arms above her head, the fluorescent lights of the lab reflecting off the polished metal surfaces. Across the workbench, a figure was hunched over an identical schematic, their brow furrowed in concentration. A sharp intake of breath escaped Elara’s lips. It was the housing design, the specific arrangement of micro-components—it was exactly the same. A sudden, hot flush crept up her neck, making her skin feel tight. She quickly lowered her gaze to her notes, trying to appear engrossed in the complex calculations, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic thumping of her own heart.

[story 2]

Mark stopped mid-sentence, the half-formed critique dying on his tongue. He slowly turned, his eyes locking onto the person seated at the adjacent station. There, bathed in the cool glow of the monitor, was a reflection of himself, albeit slightly different posture. The prototype casing, the very geometry of the internal wiring—it was a mirror image. A tremor ran through Mark’s hands as he nervously adjusted his glasses, his jaw tightening. He cleared his throat, a sound far too loud in the quiet workspace. "Interesting choice of capacitor placement," he managed, the words coming out strained and brittle. He immediately looked down at his hands, fiddling with a loose thread on his sleeve, desperate to shrink into his chair.

[story 3]

"No way," whispered Chloe, her voice barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system. She pushed her chair back slightly, the scrape of the wheels against the floor sounding deafening. The device she was assembling—the intricate layering of the polymer shell, the exact placement of the sensors—matched the one sitting just three feet away. A wave of intense unease washed over her, making her palms sweat. She began rapidly organizing her tools, stacking them with unnecessary precision, her movements jerky and too fast. She avoided looking directly at the other person, focusing instead on the smudge on the glass of her monitor, wishing the floor would simply swallow her whole.
